evidence: Packages form a clear directed acyclic hierarchy: Main → api → exploration/config/query → model/utils/viz; no reverse-direction imports detected
ConlluConverter (indexer/blacklab) depends only on java.io, java.nio, java.util — no outward dependencies to api or exploration layers
WordSketchApiServer (api) imports SemanticFieldExplorer (exploration) in its constructor — expected wiring coupling for a server bootstrap class
ExplorationHandlers (api) imports from config, exploration, and model — all downward-direction imports
api package contains 9 files (WordSketchApiServer, 4 handler classes, HttpApiUtils, ExploreResponseAssembler, RequestEntityTooLargeException) — sizeable but cohesive around HTTP concerns
No cycles found: config ← exploration ← api, query ← exploration, model ← everywhere
com.sun.net.httpserver is internal JDK API used throughout the api package — an architectural dependency on a non-stable JDK internal surface

Package structure maps to domain capabilities: api (HTTP layer), config (grammar loading/serialization), exploration (semantic algorithms), indexer (corpus ingestion), model (DTOs), query (corpus search), utils (shared primitives), viz (rendering)
model/package-info.java documents the two-tier DTO vs model-with-derived-accessors split with clear rationale
GrammarConfig (value object) and GrammarConfigLoader (file-IO machinery) separation is explicit and Javadoc-documented
GrammarConfigSerializer extracted from config value objects to keep them JSON-free — documented purpose
Handler classes are all package-private; WordSketchApiServer is the sole public API surface for the api package
ComparisonResult has both raw accessors (allAdjectives, nouns) and multiple filtered views (fullyShared, partiallyShared, specific, specificTo) — slight responsibility creep beyond pure DTO
SemanticFieldExplorer.java has ~270 lines of class-level Javadoc covering three usage modes plus example output — informative but heavier than needed
ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult rationale for class-vs-record is documented inline — trust-worthy contracts
README/CLAUDE.md structure matches observable package layout and API endpoints
Java camelCase naming conventions applied uniformly across all 43 source files — no snake_case drift
All HTTP handler classes follow XxxHandlers naming (ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, SketchHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) — consistent postfix
Internal request parameter records use XxxRequest naming (BcqlRequest, ConcordanceExamplesRequest, ValidatedExploreRequest, ValidatedSingleSeedRequest) — consistent
Options records follow XxxOptions (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions) — consistent
parse*, build*, handle* verb prefixes applied consistently across handler classes

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial handles body-too-large by calling HttpApiUtils.sendError directly and returning, whereas CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest throws RequestEntityTooLargeException — inconsistent error-handling protocol across sibling handlers
SharingCategory overrides toString() returning label; RelationEdgeType overrides toString() returning label — consistent enum pattern
PosGroup.getValue() uses get-prefix; RelationEdgeType.label(), SharingCategory.label() do not — minor get-prefix inconsistency across sibling enums

wrapWithErrorHandling in HttpApiUtils centralizes exception-to-status mapping: RequestEntityTooLargeException→413, IllegalArgumentException→400, IOException→500, Exception→500 — consistent for all wrapped handlers
ExplorationHandlers.parseRelationConfig throws IllegalArgumentException for unknown/misconfigured relations — correctly flows through wrapWithErrorHandling to 400
CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest throws RequestEntityTooLargeException for body-too-large — correctly flows through wrapWithErrorHandling to 413

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial bypasses the exception contract: calls HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 413, ...) directly and returns, instead of throwing RequestEntityTooLargeException — inconsistent with the established protocol
SketchHandlers.handleSketchRequest calls HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, "Lemma required") directly — does not throw IllegalArgumentException, bypassing wrapWithErrorHandling

ConcordanceHandlers: when no matching relation is found, bcqlQuery stays null and falls back to a proximity pattern with a logger.warn — intentional degradation but the caller receives no indication that a fallback occurred in the HTTP response (fallback:true is set in response body)
BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor converts ErrorOpeningIndex (unchecked) to IOException (checked) with context message — good error contract
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() wraps IOException in IllegalStateException with a documented two-tier contract — appropriate for startup wiring code
FetchExamplesOptions validates maxExamples >= 1 in compact constructor — good validation at data-object boundary
Domain-aligned names throughout: exploreByPattern, computeCollocateIntersection, buildCollocateToNounsMap, identifyCoreCollocates, filterByMinShared — each name describes the exact operation
parseRelationConfig, parseSeedSet, parseExplorationParams use consistent parse* prefix to signal validation + construction
buildBaseExploreResponse, buildExploreRequest, buildSingleSeedRequest use consistent build* prefix for factory-style methods
normalizedSeed (SemanticFieldExplorer) — clear intent of the variable
seedCollocScores, collocateSharedCount, nounProfiles — unambiguous composite names
ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges is overloaded for both ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult without a distinguishing suffix — the overloaded names are visually indistinguishable at call sites

CollocateQueryHelper — 'Helper' is a generic suffix; the class actually owns collocate search execution and logDice scoring, which a more precise name like CollocateSearchExecutor or CollocateRanker would convey

ExplorationResult.seed() returns a comma-joined string for multi-seed mode; seeds() returns the individual list — the existence of two similar names (seed/seeds) with different return types requires reading Javadoc to distinguish
wrapWithErrorHandling in HttpApiUtils — verb 'wrap' accurately conveys decoration pattern
SeedCollocateData (private record in MultiSeedExplorer) — clear data-bag name

QueryExecutor interface has a single production implementation (BlackLabQueryExecutor) — documented in Javadoc as 'primarily a testability seam rather than an abstraction boundary'; this is honest and appropriate self-labelling

SemanticFieldExplorer acts as a coordination facade: owns single-seed algorithm directly, delegates multi-seed to MultiSeedExplorer and comparison to CollocateProfileComparator — explicitly documented design rationale

GrammarConfigSerializer extracted from GrammarConfig/RelationConfig so config objects remain pure data carriers — appropriate extraction that prevents JSON-library coupling from leaking into the model layer
ExploreResponseAssembler owns all model-to-presentation translation for exploration endpoints — single, coherent responsibility
HttpApiUtils co-locates response-writing and request-parsing; Javadoc explicitly argues why splitting would add navigational friction without architectural benefit — defensible
RelationPatternBuilder is a pure static utility extracted from RelationConfig record so the record stays data-only — appropriate
RelationUtils is a pure static utility for alias resolution — appropriate scope
SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() is a pure pass-through: return base; — minimal value, kept as a named projection but adds no behavior or policy
ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions options-object pattern pays for itself via binary-compatible extension points and named parameters at call sites
ComparisonResult.fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), specific(), specificTo() are stream-filter views on allAdjectives() — duplicating category logic already in AdjectiveProfile.sharingCategory()

Production dependencies: blacklab-engine, blacklab-query-parser (corpus query engine), fastjson2 (JSON), slf4j-api + logback-classic (logging), jspecify (nullability annotations) — five purposeful dependencies, no duplicates
blacklab-engine and blacklab-query-parser are both used: engine for index opening/querying, query-parser for CorpusQueryLanguageParser usage in BlackLabQueryExecutor — neither is redundant
fastjson2 used for both JSON parsing (request bodies) and serialization (response bodies) — single JSON library, no Gson/Jackson mixed in
jspecify 1.0.0 — stable, lightweight annotation-only library for @NonNull/@Nullable; used in 10+ files

BlackLab 4.x is fetched from a GitHub raw-content URL (https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/) — not Maven Central; pom.xml comment acknowledges the risk explicitly
JUnit 5.9.0 (released 2022) — slightly outdated but test-scoped; no known security vulnerabilities in test scope
JUnit platform 1.9.0 matches junit 5.9.0 — version pairing is consistent
logback-classic 1.5.18 is runtime scope — correct scoping, not bundled into production API
No circular dependencies visible in the import graph — verified by tracing package imports
ConlluConverter.convertConlluToWplChunks uses long[] state = {0, 0, 0} with implicit index semantics (0=sentences, 1=tokens, 2=chunks) — magic indices instead of named fields

ConlluConverter.processLines uses Optional<BufferedWriter> to represent nullable mutable writer state — Optional is not designed for this use case; a plain @Nullable BufferedWriter field with null checks is more idiomatic Java

rotateChunkIfNeeded returns Optional<long[]> as a control signal ('rotation happened, here is the new count') — an unusual encoding; a boolean return with a mutable count parameter, or a small result record, would be clearer

GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition does custom single-pass bracket scanning (tracking depth, looking for label char + colon) — partially duplicates the bracket-walking already in CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens without delegation
RelationPatternBuilder.posGroupForPrefix uses 10+ string.contains() checks in if/else chains — a pattern→PosGroup lookup Map would be more direct and extensible

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern (the full implementation) is cleanly decomposed into 4 phase helpers (fetchSeedCollocates, buildCollocateToNounsMap, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates) with clear single responsibilities
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles nested brackets and quoted strings correctly with a depth-counting approach — complex but well-documented and appropriately tested
MultiSeedExplorer.computeCollocateIntersection is clearly decomposed into phase helpers with phase-comment headers, making the algorithm easy to follow

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExplore has an inline comment '// Safe: parseRelationConfig guarantees relationType is present' — correct but the safety depends on a cross-method invariant that the type system does not encode
ExplorationHandlers → SemanticFieldExplorer seam is clean: handlers own HTTP parameter parsing and response assembly; explorer owns domain algorithm — no HTTP concerns leak into the explorer
ExploreResponseAssembler is the single translation point from model to JSON-ready maps — centralised, no scattered ad-hoc serialization in handler methods
ValidatedExploreRequest and ValidatedSingleSeedRequest records capture the HTTP→domain parameter translation boundary cleanly
GrammarConfig (value object) + GrammarConfigLoader (file I/O) separation makes the config loading seam explicit and testable via fromReader()

buildBaseExploreResponse in ExplorationHandlers builds a partial response map; callers then add seed/seeds-specific fields before calling ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse — response assembly is spread across three call sites rather than one

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldComparison Javadoc explains why it asymmetrically does not call buildExploreRequest — correct justification, but the asymmetry itself adds seam friction for a reader learning the handler protocol

ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse takes Set<String> seeds parameter AND accesses result.nouns() for the 'seeds' response field — same data concept enters from two different sources at the same boundary
SemanticFieldExplorer is a stable facade insulating the HTTP layer from multi-seed vs single-seed vs comparison algorithm distinctions — clean seam design

ConcordanceHandlers.parseConcordanceExamplesRequest uses a private ConcordanceExamplesRequest record to cleanly capture parsed parameters before handler logic — consistent with the ValidatedXxxRequest pattern in ExplorationHandlers
Main.main() prints an ASCII banner (=== ConceptSketch - BlackLab Edition v1.5.0 ===) to stdout — decorative banner printing is a common AI-generated pattern with no functional purpose
SemanticFieldExplorer class Javadoc includes a multi-section algorithm walkthrough with a concrete 'house' example and intermediate output — this is informative WHY documentation, not restating code
model/package-info.java explains the DTO vs model-with-derived-accessors distinction with rationale — substantive design documentation
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() Javadoc describes the two-tier exception contract — explains a non-obvious design decision, appropriate

MultiSeedExplorer.computeCollocateIntersection retains '// Phase 1/2/3/4:' step comments — same pattern was removed from SemanticFieldExplorer in recent changes; the retention is inconsistent and the phase comments annotate rather than explain

SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions(): public ExplorationOptions toBaseOptions() { return base; } — a pure pass-through method with no added behavior, policy, or translation; matches AI-generated 'helper method' pattern
GrammarConfigLoader.missingConfigException is a private one-line method that centralises an error message — minimal extraction value for a method called from a single site
RelationEdgeType.toString() override Javadoc explains that it overrides Object.toString() to emit API-safe label — the explanation is appropriate for a non-obvious Java decision
No restating comments found (// increment i above i++ style) across the codebase
No defensive null checks on @NonNull-annotated parameters beyond the appropriate Objects.requireNonNull at constructor/factory boundaries
No generic domain names (handleData, processItem) — all names are domain-specific
WordSketchApiServer.java line 96: TODO comment 'remove in a future version after clients have migrated to /api/semantic-field/compare' on the /api/semantic-field legacy alias route
GrammarConfigLoader.validateNoLegacyKeys() actively rejects the deprecated 'copulas' top-level key with an IOException, enforcing migration away from the old config format
GrammarConfigLoader: 'cql_pattern' field removed — Javadoc says 'canonical field name is pattern; cql_pattern is no longer supported' with a validation error for absent 'pattern'
No @Deprecated annotations found on any called symbols in the production code
No mixed old/new API style patterns (e.g. old procedural vs new record-based handlers) — the codebase is uniformly modern Java 17+ records and sealed patterns throughout
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES maps short aliases to canonical IDs — these are intentional and current, not migration shims
The TODO on the legacy alias has no associated ticket number or target version, reducing traceability
incomplete_migration.issues_preventing_higher_score: One legacy API alias (/api/semantic-field) is retained with a TODO to remove it, but the TODO lacks a version or ticket reference making the migration timeline untraceable.

evidence: Clear top-level package split: api, config, exploration, indexer, model, query, utils, viz — each corresponds to a coherent concern
model/package-info.java explicitly documents the package boundary: 'No persistence, I/O, or HTTP concerns belong in this package'
ExploreResponseAssembler correctly lives in api, not model — it depends on HttpExchange context and Edge serialization, making the api placement correct
CollocateProfileComparator and MultiSeedExplorer are package-private in exploration, correctly hidden from callers who only need SemanticFieldExplorer as the facade
GrammarConfigSerializer extracted to config package rather than being a method on GrammarConfig — keeps the value object dependency-free of fastjson2
VisualizationHandlers in api, RadialPlot in viz — the split is clean but RadialPlot is purely a stateless SVG builder; moving it to utils or keeping it in viz are both defensible
BlackLabConllUIndexer and ConlluConverter share indexer/blacklab subpackage, correctly grouped with indexing concerns
CqlUtils and LogDiceCalculator live in utils, accessed by multiple packages — appropriate for shared primitives

api package hosts handler classes for distinct concerns (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers) alongside infrastructure (HttpApiUtils, WordSketchApiServer) — slightly crowded but functionally coherent
buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution: Javadoc says 'cqlPattern must start with ['; throws IAE on any other format — honored by implementation
parseBcqlRequest: Javadoc documents all three exception types (IAE for 400-class, RequestEntityTooLargeException for 413, IOException for I/O) — wrapWithErrorHandling fulfills this contract
requireParam: documented to throw IAE when missing/empty; wrapWithErrorHandling catches IAE and maps to 400 — contract is end-to-end coherent
executeCollocations accepts @Nullable String lemma and documents that it 'returns an empty list silently if null or empty' — implemented as documented

ExplorationResult constructor does not defensively copy seedCollocates, seedCollocateFrequencies, discoveredNouns, or coreCollocates — the class Javadoc claims 'All collections are non-null' but callers can mutate the internal state by holding a reference to the passed-in map

CollocateQueryHelper(@Nullable BlackLabIndex index): Javadoc says 'Null is accepted to support test subclasses'; but getTotalFrequency() throws NPE-via-requireNonNull when called with null — this is partially documented but the 'null is safe' implication is misleading for any method that actually needs the index
compareCollocateProfiles returns ComparisonResult.empty() for null/fewer-than-2 seeds without logging — callers that pass a single-element set receive an empty result with no indication of why
SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern convenience overload: throws IAE for relationType.isEmpty() and for collocatePosGroup==OTHER — these preconditions are not documented in the method Javadoc

GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader: Javadoc says 'throws IOException if the reader fails or the JSON is invalid' — fulfilled but fromReader also wraps all JSON parse errors, so the contract is broader than stated
ConlluConverter: processLines calls writerOpt.get() inside an inSentence-guarded block — safe but the invariant (inSentence==true implies writerOpt.isPresent()) is implicit and not documented

ConlluConverter.processLines: calls writerOpt.get() at line 'writerOpt.get().write("</s>\n")' inside an 'if (inSentence)' block — the invariant 'inSentence==true implies writerOpt.isPresent()' is implicit and requires tracing the control flow to verify

ConlluConverter: 'Optional<long[]> rotated = rotateChunkIfNeeded(...)' returns a long[] packed inside Optional solely to signal whether rotation occurred — a dedicated record or a boolean+side-effect would be clearer

GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition: counts complete [..] bracket groups before the first 'targetLabel:' substring in a character-by-character loop — correct but dense; the outer label-finding and inner token-counting passes are interleaved in one method

MultiSeedExplorer: Phase 3 builds aggregate score/frequency maps in-line in computeCollocateIntersection before delegating to phase helpers — phases 3b-3c (buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates) are in separate methods but 3a is inline, creating an inconsistent phase boundary

ExplorationHandlers.handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: nouns_per preflight check is done before buildExploreRequest but after parseQueryParams — the ordering dependency is not obvious from reading the method

CollocateProfileComparator.buildAdjectiveProfileList: two-pass algorithm (single-pass for sum/min/max, second pass for variance) is clearly commented with 'Second pass for variance (requires the mean — unavoidable two-pass for numerical stability)'
BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution: the else branch throws IAE with 'Unrecognized CQL pattern format' — clean and clear

SemanticFieldExplorer.exploreByPattern: 'if (seed.isEmpty()) return ExplorationResult.empty("")' early return is clear, but the normalizedSeed variable is created after the empty check — minor ordering issue, not a logic bug
identifyCommonCollocates: 'int threshold = Math.min(minShared, seedCount)' — correct clamping logic, clearly named

VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial: 413 check is done with 'if (bodyBytes.length > MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES) { HttpApiUtils.sendError(...); return; }' — does not throw RequestEntityTooLargeException like CorpusQueryHandlers does, inconsistent pattern for body-size enforcement
jspecify @NonNull/@Nullable annotations used throughout: ExplorationHandlers, HttpApiUtils, SemanticFieldExplorer, MultiSeedExplorer, QueryExecutor — consistent adoption
GrammarConfig.getVersion() annotated @NonNull — the loader guarantees a non-blank version string or throws IOException, so the annotation matches runtime behavior
GrammarConfig.getConfigPath() annotated @Nullable — correct since fromReader() passes null
RelationConfig.computeDeprel() returns @Nullable String — correct, returns null for non-DEP relations
QueryExecutor.executeCollocations accepts @Nullable String lemma — annotation matches the 'returns empty list silently if null' documented behavior

ExplorationResult stores seedCollocates and seedCollocateFrequencies as untyped Map<String, Double>/Map<String, Long> without defensive copying — the runtime type is correct but mutation is possible, so @NonNull return annotations on seedCollocates() and seedCollocateFrequencies() over-promise stability

HttpApiUtils.parseQueryParams returns @NonNull Map but the map is a plain HashMap that callers can mutate freely — not a safety issue for this use but the annotation implies stability it does not provide

CollocateQueryHelper constructor accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex — correctly annotated but multiple methods (executeCollocateSearch, buildAndRankCollocates) call index.method() without null-checking, relying on requireNonNull only in getTotalFrequency; null index causes NPE in other methods
Map<String, Object> used throughout response assembly — necessary for dynamic JSON but erases type information; no generic wrapper around the map keys used (e.g. enum keys)
BlackLabQueryExecutor.buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution is package-private (not in interface) — missing @Nullable/@NonNull on the static method parameters, though it is tested
ExplorationOptions and SingleSeedExplorationOptions are records — all fields are primitive or known non-null, no annotation needed; correctly designed
FetchExamplesOptions record (maxExamples int) — primitive, fine

ExplorationHandlers uses three private helper layers: parameter parsing (buildSingleSeedRequest, buildExploreRequest, parseRelationConfig, parseExplorationParams), response building (buildBaseExploreResponse), and delegation to SemanticFieldExplorer — each layer has a single purpose

Private records ValidatedSingleSeedRequest and ValidatedExploreRequest encapsulate parsed request state rather than passing multiple parameters through handler methods — correct use of records as typed bundles

ExplorationParams record correctly carries all four numeric parameters; however, it includes 'nounsPerCollocate' even for the multi-seed path where it is irrelevant — the field is pre-flight rejected in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti but still parsed

handleSemanticFieldComparison explicitly documents its asymmetry with handleSemanticFieldExplore and handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: 'unlike ... this handler intentionally does not call buildExploreRequest because comparison is cross-relational' — the asymmetry is justified and documented
parseSeedSet is a pure static method with no side effects, correctly placed as private static
parseRelationConfig throws IAE on unknown relation or missing relationType — this is the correct exception for wrapWithErrorHandling to catch
buildBaseExploreResponse constructs the shared response skeleton and merges extraParams — avoids duplication between single-seed and multi-seed response assembly

The guard 'if (params.containsKey("nouns_per")) throw IAE' in handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti is applied before buildExploreRequest — but parseExplorationParams in buildExploreRequest still parses nouns_per with a default value anyway; the guard makes the contract clear but the parse is redundant
SemanticFieldExplorer injected as a field rather than constructed inside handlers — enables testing via the full-constructor path in WordSketchApiServer
All handler methods are package-private, correctly limiting visibility to the api package and the server wiring

BlackLabQueryExecutor constructor opens the BlackLab index — documented as intentional: 'opening the index is inherent to BlackLab's lifecycle model'; constructor declares throws IOException so callers get a checked exception rather than a deferred runtime failure
WordSketchApiServer constructor builds all handler instances (SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, etc.) but defers port binding to start() — correct separation of construction and network I/O
Main.handleBlackLabServerCommand: shutdown hook added before server construction — ensures executor is always closed even if server construction throws; comment explicitly explains this ordering
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish() reads System.getProperty at call time, not at class load — no class-initialization side effects
HttpApiUtils.setCorsHeader() reads System.getProperty at call time — per-call reads allow per-test overrides to take effect immediately, documented in code
SemanticFieldExplorer constructor initializes three fields (comparator, multiSeedExplorer, nounCqlConstraint) with no I/O — pure construction
CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable index — allows test construction without I/O but introduces the partial-guard issue noted in type_safety
WordSketchApiServer has two constructors: convenience constructor creates SemanticFieldExplorer internally; full constructor accepts injected explorer for testing — clean DI seam without global state
No static initializer blocks with I/O or side effects found in any production class
No global mutable singletons found — all state is held in instance fields with clear ownership
ConlluConverter: static Pattern MWT_OR_EMPTY compiled at class load — benign, patterns are compile-time constants
initialization_coupling.issues_preventing_higher_score: BlackLabQueryExecutor performs I/O in its constructor (opening the index), which is documented as intentional but means the object cannot be constructed without a live index. This prevents mock-construction without subclassing and makes construction order visible to callers.

evidence: 27 test files present covering: config loading/validation, CQL utils, logDice calculator, query executor static helpers, snippet parsing, collocate query helper, exploration algorithms, response assembler, and HTTP handler behavior

BlackLabQueryExecutorTest splits CI-safe static helper tests (no index required, always run) from live-index tests (@Disabled + Assumptions.assumeTrue) — correct strategy for an index-dependent codebase
SemanticFieldExplorerInternalTest and MultiSeedExplorerTest use stub executors (inline anonymous classes) that return canned data — tests the domain algorithm in isolation from BlackLab
CollocateProfileComparatorTest and HandlersTest use stub/mock patterns consistently
GrammarConfigLoaderTest and RelationsValidationTest cover invalid-config rejection paths (missing version, duplicate id, copulas key) — critical paths for startup correctness
WrapHandlerTest tests the error-mapping contract of wrapWithErrorHandling independently
CorpusQueryHandlers has no dedicated test file — the 413 body-size limit, bracket-depth limit, and JSON parse error paths are tested only through HandlersTest (which focuses on 400-class paths)
VisualizationHandlers has no dedicated test file — the radial SVG generation path is covered only by RadialPlotTest (tests the SVG builder directly, not the HTTP handler)
ConcordanceHandlers.handleConcordanceExamples fallback logic (when relation not found) is not explicitly tested — HandlersTest only covers missing-parameter 400 responses
ConlluConverterTest exists — covers the chunk rotation and sentence counting paths; the Optional<BufferedWriter> invariant is exercised implicitly but not explicitly tested
BlackLabConllUIndexerTest is present but marked @Disabled for CI environments without an index — live integration coverage is good when enabled
ExploreResponseAssemblerTest covers edge/format serialization — important since assembler is the single point of model-to-JSON translation
All GET endpoints use registerGetHandler; the one mutating endpoint (POST /api/bcql) uses registerPostHandler — HTTP method semantics are correct and consistently enforced
All success responses include 'status': 'ok' as the first field — consistent acknowledgment shape
All error responses use the same {'error': '...'} shape via HttpApiUtils.sendError — uniform error envelope
wrapWithErrorHandling applied to every non-health endpoint — no endpoint bypasses the IAE→400/RETLE→413/IOException→500 mapping

Parameter naming: single-seed uses 'seed'; multi-seed uses 'seeds'; comparison uses 'seeds'; concordance examples uses 'seed' and 'collocate' — 'seed' is used for different cardinalities depending on endpoint, which is correct but relies on the caller reading per-endpoint docs to know the cardinality constraint
ConcordanceHandlers Javadoc: 'formerly word1 / formerly word2' — old parameter names are documented but no API-level deprecation header is emitted for clients using the old names

Explore endpoints (/explore, /explore-multi, /compare) return different response shapes: explore adds 'seed' field, explore-multi adds 'seeds'/'seed_count', compare adds 'seeds'/'seed_count'/'fully_shared_count'/'partially_shared_count'/'specific_count' — shapes are appropriately differentiated but not documented in a machine-readable schema

POST /api/bcql returns 'status', 'query', 'total_results', 'top', 'results' — shape is different from sketch endpoints which return 'status', 'lemma', 'relations' — this is appropriate given different semantics but the 'status' field is the only shared key
VisualizationHandlers returns SVG (image/svg+xml) not JSON — the single non-JSON response type; correct but callers must know this endpoint differs

/health endpoint does not go through wrapWithErrorHandling and has no CORS preflight handling through registerGetHandler — it uses a lambda directly and still has setCorsHeader applied via sendJsonResponse, but lacks OPTIONS response
WordSketchApiServer binds exclusively to InetAddress.getLoopbackAddress() (127.0.0.1) — no external network exposure
Class Javadoc explicitly documents the security model: 'All endpoints are unauthenticated — access control is provided by the host firewall and the loopback-only bind'
Javadoc warns: 'Any change to the bind address (e.g., to 0.0.0.0 for container deployment) must be accompanied by an authentication or network-level access-control layer'
All 12 registered endpoints apply the same access control: none (loopback-only by bind address) — the model is consistently applied across all endpoints
POST /api/bcql accepts arbitrary BCQL patterns; access control is by loopback bind, not by input validation for auth purposes; input validation is for size/complexity only
No CSRF protection on POST endpoints — consistent with the loopback-only, single-user tool model
No per-endpoint authorization checks, roles, or permission decorators — intentional for a local research tool
No authentication headers, API keys, or session tokens used anywhere — consistent with documented design
Rate limiting: none; consistent — the tool is designed for controlled local use, not multi-tenant access
CORS: Access-Control-Allow-Origin defaults to 'http://localhost:3000', configurable via system property — correctly limits cross-origin access to local webapp only
authorization_consistency.issues_preventing_higher_score: The security model is explicit and consistently applied but depends entirely on the loopback bind; there is no defense-in-depth if the bind address is changed for container deployment. The documented warning is present but not machine-enforced.

strengths: Clear directed layer hierarchy with no detected import cycles
ConlluConverter is fully self-contained within the indexer subpackage with no cross-layer leakage
model package is a pure DTO sink — nothing in model imports from api or exploration
Handler classes are package-private, with WordSketchApiServer as the sole public entry point to the api package
cross_module_architecture.issue_character: The architecture is sound and cycle-free; the primary concern is a dependency on the internal JDK com.sun.net.httpserver API throughout the api package, which is not a cross-layer boundary issue but an external dependency stability concern.
cross_module_architecture.score_rationale: The codebase has clearly defined and respected layer boundaries with no cycles. The main architectural weakness is the reliance on com.sun.net.httpserver (internal JDK API) as the HTTP primitive, which is a platform coupling risk but not a cross-module architecture violation. Minor: the api package is moderately large but sub-divided logically. Scores in the low-80s — above a typical mid-level codebase but with the JDK internal dependency preventing a high-90s score.

strengths: Package names directly mirror domain capabilities, not technical concerns
GrammarConfig/GrammarConfigLoader separation is explicit and Javadoc-motivated
model package is well-bounded with no persistence or HTTP concerns
Public API surface is minimal: handler classes are package-private, one public server entry point
high_level_elegance.issue_character: The decomposition is mostly clean; the main issue is mild responsibility creep in the model layer — ComparisonResult accumulates filtered-view methods that belong in a service or assembler — and the exploration package's facade (SemanticFieldExplorer) has a disproportionately heavy Javadoc relative to what it coordinates.
high_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase scores well on package-to-capability alignment and explicit architectural contracts. It is held back from the high 80s by ComparisonResult accreting query-convenience methods beyond pure-DTO territory, and by SemanticFieldExplorer's class-level documentation being denser than the coordination code it introduces. A new engineer can understand the structure without surprises, but the model layer's boundary is slightly soft.

strengths: Java naming conventions (camelCase, PascalCase for types) applied uniformly with no snake_case islands
Handler classes and their internal request/options records follow consistent naming patterns
Enum types consistently implement label() + toString() override for API-safe serialization
parse*, build*, handle* verb prefixes are codebase-wide conventions, not local patterns
convention_outlier.issue_character: Conventions are broadly consistent; the one clear outlier is VisualizationHandlers breaking the exception-based error signalling protocol that all other handlers follow for body-too-large conditions.
convention_outlier.score_rationale: The codebase is well-conventioned at the naming and structural levels. The score is held out of the mid-80s by the single handler that deviates from the established RequestEntityTooLargeException protocol, and by PosGroup.getValue() using a get-prefix while sibling enums RelationEdgeType and SharingCategory use the un-prefixed label() convention.

strengths: wrapWithErrorHandling establishes a clear error-to-status mapping contract at the API boundary
IllegalArgumentException as the 400-class error signal is used consistently across most handlers
BlackLabQueryExecutor converts unchecked RuntimeException to checked IOException with preserved context
GrammarConfigLoader.createDefaultEnglish two-tier exception contract is explicitly documented
error_consistency.issue_character: Two handler methods bypass the established exception-based error contract by calling HttpApiUtils.sendError directly — VisualizationHandlers for body-too-large and SketchHandlers for missing lemma — creating an inconsistent mixture of throw-based and direct-response error patterns.
error_consistency.score_rationale: The codebase has a clear centralized error-handling strategy via wrapWithErrorHandling that most of the code follows. The score is held in the low 70s because two handler methods silently bypass this contract (direct sendError calls), meaning subtle future changes to error handling could affect some handlers but not others. These are concrete consistency failures, not just stylistic preferences.

strengths: Domain vocabulary (collocate, logDice, seed, lemma, relation) used precisely and consistently throughout
Method names are verb-phrase accurate: computeCollocateIntersection, filterByMinShared, identifyCoreCollocates
parse*/build*/handle* verb prefixes are codebase-wide conventions clearly signalling method roles
Variable names are descriptive composites (seedCollocScores, nounProfiles, collocateSharedCount)
naming_quality.issue_character: The codebase names are generally excellent; the handful of issues are mild precision losses — one overloaded method where the two variants are indistinguishable by signature alone, one 'Helper' class with a more specific role than the name implies, and the seed/seeds pair requiring documentation to distinguish.
naming_quality.score_rationale: Naming quality is well above average — domain terms are used precisely and method verb prefixes are consistent across the codebase. The score stays in the low 80s rather than the high 80s due to CollocateQueryHelper's imprecise suffix, the ambiguous buildEdges overload, and the seed/seeds pair needing Javadoc to distinguish. These are minor friction points, not readability blockers.

strengths: QueryExecutor interface is honest about being a testability seam — does not pretend to be a port with multiple implementations
GrammarConfigSerializer/RelationPatternBuilder/RelationUtils are all correctly extracted as pure static utilities with no artificial object overhead
SemanticFieldExplorer facade is explicitly documented with its coordination role and stability contract
ExploreResponseAssembler is a genuine separation of concerns — it owns the model-to-JSON translation and nothing else
abstraction_fitness.issue_character: Abstractions in this codebase generally earn their keep; the main fitness issue is ComparisonResult accumulating stream-filter views that duplicate logic already in AdjectiveProfile, and SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() being a pure return pass-through that adds a name without adding behavior.
abstraction_fitness.score_rationale: Most abstractions in the codebase are appropriately scoped and earn their indirection through genuine leverage. The score is in the low 80s because a small number of pass-throughs and duplicated filter views add surface without value. No delegation-heavy classes or facade-only re-export modules were found.

strengths: Minimal dependency footprint: 5 production dependencies, all purposeful
No duplicate libraries for the same purpose (single JSON library, single logging façade)
jspecify used correctly as a lightweight annotation dependency without pulling in heavy annotation processors
logback-classic correctly scoped to runtime to avoid it leaking into compile classpath
dependency_health.issue_character: Dependencies are lean and well-chosen; the primary health concern is the BlackLab 4.x dependency resolved from an unstable GitHub raw-content URL with no availability SLA, which is a CI reliability and supply-chain risk.
dependency_health.score_rationale: The codebase has an admirably lean dependency set with no duplication or unnecessary heavy pulls. The score is held in the upper 70s rather than the mid-80s solely because of the BlackLab GitHub raw-content repository URL, which the pom.xml itself flags as fragile. This is a real infrastructure risk — a repository rename, rate limit, or branch deletion would break CI with no fallback. JUnit 5.9.0 being slightly dated is a minor secondary concern.

strengths: SemanticFieldExplorer algorithm decomposed cleanly into named phase helpers with single responsibilities
MultiSeedExplorer phase helpers (fetchCollocatesPerSeed, identifyCommonCollocates, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates) are intention-revealing
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens handles edge cases (nested brackets, quoted strings) correctly with clear state tracking
GrammarConfigLoader private methods are appropriately sized and named with no monolithic parse method
low_level_elegance.issue_character: The main low-level elegance issues are concentrated in ConlluConverter, which uses Optional<T> to model mutable nullable state (a non-idiomatic Java pattern) and a raw indexed array for multi-field state — both of which obscure intent and create fragile implicit contracts.
low_level_elegance.score_rationale: The majority of the codebase is well-structured at the function level with clean decomposition and clear state management. The score is in the low 70s because ConlluConverter contains two distinct implementation-craft issues (Optional as nullable-state holder, indexed long[] for named state), GrammarConfigLoader has a bracket-walking reimplementation that partially duplicates CqlUtils, and RelationPatternBuilder has a string-matching if-chain where a lookup table would be cleaner. These are concrete readability and correctness-risk issues, not style preferences.

strengths: ExploreResponseAssembler provides a single, coherent model-to-presentation translation boundary with no scattered ad-hoc maps in handlers
ValidatedXxxRequest / XxxRequest private records capture the HTTP→domain translation at a well-defined point
GrammarConfigLoader.fromReader() provides a clean testability seam that avoids file-system coupling in tests
SemanticFieldExplorer facade insulates the HTTP layer from changes to the three algorithm implementations behind it
mid_level_elegance.issue_character: Seam design is mostly clean; the primary issues are spread response-assembly (base map built in one place, extended in another, populated in a third) and a data duplication where populateComparisonResponse receives seeds from two sources.
mid_level_elegance.score_rationale: The codebase handles most seams well — the ExploreResponseAssembler boundary is clean, handler-to-explorer communication is explicit, and the grammar config seam is well-designed. The score is held in the mid 70s because the explore response is assembled across multiple call sites (buildBaseExploreResponse + caller adds seed fields + populateExploreResponse), and the comparison handler's asymmetric protocol requires inline documentation to explain. These create non-trivial cognitive load when tracing the data flow for a new contributor.

strengths: No restating comments (code-echoing) found anywhere in the production codebase
Domain names are specific and precise — no handleData, processItem, doOperation generics
No defensive null checks on non-nullable typed values beyond appropriate entry boundaries
Most Javadoc explains WHY rather than WHAT — design decisions, constraints, and non-obvious choices are documented
ai_generated_debt.issue_character: The codebase has low AI-generated debt; the issues present are minor: a decorative stdout banner, one pure pass-through method that survived a cleanup pass, and phase-step comments in MultiSeedExplorer that annotate rather than explain.
ai_generated_debt.score_rationale: The codebase demonstrates clear evidence of thoughtful human authorship — substantive design documentation, precise naming, and no code-echoing comments. The score is in the mid 70s rather than the high 80s because of a small cluster of AI-hallmark patterns: the stdout banner, the toBaseOptions() pass-through that should have been removed in the same cleanup that removed the other three delegation methods, and the Phase 1-4 step comments in MultiSeedExplorer that were removed elsewhere but remained here.

strengths: Active rejection of deprecated config keys ('copulas') at load time prevents drift back to old patterns
No @Deprecated symbols called by active production code — the codebase has a clean call graph
Migration from 'cql_pattern' to 'pattern' is complete and enforced by a validation error rather than a silent fallback
RelationUtils.RELATION_ALIASES is clearly documented as intentional alias mapping, not a migration shim
incomplete_migration.issue_character: A single intentional backward-compatibility shim (legacy API path /api/semantic-field) is retained without a version or tracking reference, creating a low-risk but untracked migration debt.
incomplete_migration.score_rationale: The codebase is nearly complete in migration terms: one legacy HTTP alias is the only coexistence artifact, and it is clearly labeled. The absence of a version or ticket number on the TODO is the sole gap. This is equivalent to a project that has cleaned up most debt but left one loose end without a deadline.

strengths: model/package-info.java formally documents the no-I/O, no-HTTP boundary for the model package
Package-private visibility for internal exploration workers (CollocateProfileComparator, MultiSeedExplorer) correctly prevents callers from bypassing the SemanticFieldExplorer facade
Serialization concerns (GrammarConfigSerializer, ExploreResponseAssembler) are separated from pure data objects
Indexer subpackage grouping (indexer/blacklab) anticipates future indexer implementations without polluting the root
package_organization.issue_character: The api package is somewhat monolithic — it conflates HTTP infrastructure (server, utils, error types) with request handling (five handler classes and a response assembler), but all within an acceptably small surface area.
package_organization.score_rationale: The overall package layout is intentional and well-reasoned, with explicit documentation of boundaries. The main limitation is that the api package blurs infrastructure and handler concerns, and the viz package contains only one class that could live in utils. These are navigational rather than architectural problems.

strengths: wrapWithErrorHandling provides a consistent and documented error-mapping contract for all HTTP handlers
buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution has a precise precondition and throws IAE — the contract is tight and testable
requireParam, parseIntParam, parseDoubleParam all document and honor the same IAE-for-missing-input contract
QueryExecutor interface method contracts are detailed and distinguish executeCollocations/executeSurfacePattern/executeBcqlQuery semantics
contract_coherence.issue_character: The primary contract gap is mutable exposure: ExplorationResult stores caller-supplied collections by reference without defensive copying, undermining the 'all collections non-null' invariant documented in its Javadoc. Secondary gaps are implicit invariants in ConlluConverter and undocumented preconditions in SemanticFieldExplorer.
contract_coherence.score_rationale: The HTTP-layer contracts are well-specified and honored end-to-end. Domain-layer contracts have two meaningful gaps: ExplorationResult does not defend its stated immutability guarantee, and CollocateProfileComparator silently returns empty on invalid input without any indication to the caller. These gaps are real but localized.

strengths: Two-pass variance algorithm in CollocateProfileComparator is explicitly justified in a comment
buildBcqlWithLemmaSubstitution has a clean, provably-correct if/else with no hidden branches
identifyCommonCollocates applies threshold clamping with clear intent
Phase structure in MultiSeedExplorer is documented with phase comments and extracted into methods
logic_clarity.issue_character: Logic correctness is generally sound, but several non-obvious invariants (the Optional<writer>/inSentence coupling, inline vs extracted phase steps, inconsistent 413-throwing patterns) require cross-method mental tracking to verify, raising the cost of future changes.
logic_clarity.score_rationale: The logic is provably correct throughout but relies on implicit invariants rather than explicit ones in a few key places. The Optional-based writer pattern in ConlluConverter and the inline phase in MultiSeedExplorer are the highest-friction areas. The 413 enforcement inconsistency (throw vs sendError) is a minor but real clarity gap.

strengths: jspecify annotations consistently adopted in public/package APIs — most null contracts are explicit and correct
Record types (ExplorationOptions, SingleSeedExplorationOptions, FetchExamplesOptions, BcqlRequest, ConcordanceExamplesRequest) provide correct-by-construction type safety for request parameters
GrammarConfig and RelationConfig are final/immutable value objects with @NonNull/@Nullable annotations matching their loader guarantees
type_safety.issue_character: Type safety annotations are generally correct for the values they annotate, but two systematic gaps exist: CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable index yet exposes methods that NPE for null, and ExplorationResult's @NonNull collection accessors over-promise stability because the underlying maps are not defensively copied.
type_safety.score_rationale: The codebase has meaningfully adopted nullability annotations but has two structural weaknesses: an injectable null that causes runtime NPEs beyond the one guarded method, and a mismatch between @NonNull collection annotations and the absence of defensive copies. Together these reduce the practical value of the annotations in those areas.

strengths: Three-layer handler structure (parse → delegate → respond) is consistent across all handlers in ExplorationHandlers
Private records for request bundles (ValidatedSingleSeedRequest, ValidatedExploreRequest, ExplorationParams) provide typed, self-documenting parameter passing
Documented asymmetry in handleSemanticFieldComparison with explicit rationale prevents a future refactor from incorrectly 'fixing' the intentional deviation
SemanticFieldExplorer injected as a dependency, enabling constructor-based testing seam
design_coherence.issue_character: The design is structurally sound with one recurring minor inconsistency: ExplorationParams carries nounsPerCollocate for all paths including multi-seed where it is explicitly disallowed, and the guard and the parse are both present but not coordinated.
design_coherence.score_rationale: ExplorationHandlers demonstrates deliberately structured design: concerns are separated into layers, request state is typed, and asymmetries are documented. The one design smell (nouns_per parsed but rejected, redundant in multi-seed path) is contained and non-breaking. The overall handler is a good example of the request-response handler pattern applied consistently.

strengths: Shutdown hook is registered before server construction, preventing resource leaks on construction failures
Port binding deferred to start() in WordSketchApiServer — construction and network binding are cleanly separated
No static initializer blocks with I/O or mutable global singletons anywhere in the codebase
System.getProperty reads are deferred to call time (not class load), enabling per-test overrides without JVM restart
initialization_coupling.issue_character: Initialization coupling is minimal and well-controlled; the only structural coupling is that BlackLabQueryExecutor performs I/O in its constructor by design, which is documented and intentional given BlackLab's lifecycle model.
initialization_coupling.score_rationale: The initialization story is mature: construction vs binding separation in WordSketchApiServer, deferred property reads in HttpApiUtils, and explicit shutdown-hook ordering in Main all reflect deliberate design decisions. The constructor I/O in BlackLabQueryExecutor is the only non-ideal pattern but is correctly documented and unavoidable given the library contract.

strengths: Clear CI/live-index split with @Disabled + Assumptions.assumeTrue prevents flaky CI failures from index-dependent tests
Stub executor pattern used consistently in exploration tests — enables fast, deterministic algorithm testing without BlackLab
Config rejection paths (copulas key, missing version, duplicate id) are explicitly tested — startup correctness is well-guarded
WrapHandlerTest independently verifies the error-mapping contract for all exception types (IAE → 400, RETLE → 413, IOException → 500)
test_strategy.issue_character: The HTTP handler layer is undertested at the integration level: CorpusQueryHandlers (413 body-size, bracket-depth limit) and VisualizationHandlers (body-size and parse errors) lack dedicated tests for their critical validation paths.
test_strategy.score_rationale: Algorithm and config tests are well-structured and cover the key correctness properties. The main gap is in HTTP handler validation paths: CorpusQueryHandlers has no test for its 413/bracket-depth guards, and ConcordanceHandlers fallback logic is not covered. Given that the HTTP handlers are the external API boundary, these omissions matter for regression safety.

strengths: Consistent error envelope: all error responses use {'error': '...'} shape via a single sendError method
wrapWithErrorHandling applied uniformly to all non-trivial endpoints
HTTP method enforcement is consistent: registerGetHandler for reads, registerPostHandler for the BCQL mutation
CORS preflight handling is centralized in registerHandler and applies to all registered routes
api_surface_coherence.issue_character: The API surface is mostly coherent; the main friction is the 'seed' parameter name used for single-cardinality endpoints vs 'seeds' for multi-cardinality endpoints, and the /health endpoint not going through the standard route registration path.
api_surface_coherence.score_rationale: The API surface has a clear and consistent structural backbone (unified error format, wrapWithErrorHandling, HTTP method enforcement). The response shape differentiation between endpoints is appropriate but informal — no machine-readable schema. The /health endpoint is a minor outlier lacking OPTIONS handling through the standard path.

strengths: Authorization model is explicitly documented in class Javadoc with clear rationale and an explicit deployment warning
Loopback-only bind is consistently enforced for all endpoints via a single constructor — no per-endpoint override possible
CORS default restricts cross-origin access to the local webapp port (3000), providing a second layer of browser-level access control
The documented warning about container deployment is actionable: it names the specific risk (0.0.0.0 bind) and the mitigation required (auth or network-level ACL)
authorization_consistency.issue_character: The authorization model is intentionally minimal (loopback-only, no auth) and is consistently applied across all endpoints; there is no inconsistency to find. The sole risk is that the loopback bind is a deployment-time constraint with no runtime enforcement.
authorization_consistency.score_rationale: Authorization consistency is high: the choice is explicit, documented, and uniformly applied. The score is not higher because the documented security model is a single-layer defense (network bind) with no fallback, and the CORS allow-origin is a static default that must be overridden via system property for production deployments — a configuration risk rather than a code inconsistency.

issues.summary: api package depends on com.sun.net.httpserver, an internal JDK API not guaranteed stable across Java versions
issues.suggestion: Introduce a thin HttpServer abstraction or switch to a standard embedded HTTP server (e.g. Jetty embedded, or the standard-module com.sun.net.httpserver via explicit --add-exports in module-info if staying on JDK). If staying with com.sun, add --add-exports to build config to make the dependency explicit and testable across Java versions.
evidence: WordSketchApiServer uses com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpServer directly in start(), registerHandler(), registerGetHandler(), registerPostHandler()
HttpApiUtils method signatures accept com.sun.net.httpserver.HttpExchange as parameters
com.sun.net.httpserver is in the jdk.httpserver module which is not part of the public Java SE API spec

issues.summary: ComparisonResult accumulates multiple filtered-view methods that belong in an assembler or service layer
issues.suggestion: Move fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), specific(), and specificTo() out of ComparisonResult into ExploreResponseAssembler or a dedicated ComparisonResultView helper. Keep ComparisonResult as a plain carrier with nouns(), allAdjectives(), summaryCounts(), and empty().
evidence: ComparisonResult exposes fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), specific(), and specificTo(noun) — four stream-filter methods in a model DTO
These views duplicate the category logic already in AdjectiveProfile.sharingCategory()
The class comment says 'No persistence, I/O, or HTTP concerns belong in this package' — the filtered views are presentation-layer logic that crept into the model

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial handles body-too-large by calling sendError directly instead of throwing RequestEntityTooLargeException like sibling handlers
issues.suggestion: In VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial, replace the direct sendError call with throw new RequestEntityTooLargeException("Request body too large") to match the protocol established by CorpusQueryHandlers.
evidence: CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest: throws new RequestEntityTooLargeException("Request body too large") — flows through wrapWithErrorHandling → 413
VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial: calls HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 413, "Request body too large"); return; — bypasses the exception-based contract
Both handlers are registered with wrapWithErrorHandling in WordSketchApiServer.registerRoutes()

issues.summary: PosGroup.getValue() uses get-prefix accessor while sibling enums RelationEdgeType and SharingCategory use unprefixed label()
issues.suggestion: Rename PosGroup.getValue() to PosGroup.label() (or value()) to match the label() convention used by RelationEdgeType and SharingCategory. Update the single call site in GrammarConfigSerializer or wherever getValue() is used.
evidence: PosGroup: public String getValue() { return value; }
RelationEdgeType: public String label() { return label; }
SharingCategory: public String label() { return label; }

issues.summary: SketchHandlers.handleSketchRequest sends a 400 response directly instead of throwing IllegalArgumentException
issues.suggestion: Replace the direct sendError call with throw new IllegalArgumentException("Lemma required") so the error flows through wrapWithErrorHandling consistently with all other handlers.
evidence: handleSketchRequest: if (parts.length == 0 || parts[0].isEmpty()) { HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 400, "Lemma required"); return; }
All other handlers signal 400 by throwing IllegalArgumentException caught by wrapWithErrorHandling
This means handleSketchRequest's error path is invisible to any future centralised error-handling middleware added to wrapWithErrorHandling

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers.handleVisualRadial handles body-too-large by calling sendError directly instead of throwing RequestEntityTooLargeException
issues.suggestion: Replace the direct sendError+return with throw new RequestEntityTooLargeException("Request body too large") to match CorpusQueryHandlers and route through wrapWithErrorHandling.
evidence: handleVisualRadial: if (bodyBytes.length > MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES) { HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 413, "Request body too large"); return; }
CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest: throw new RequestEntityTooLargeException("Request body too large") — the established protocol

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler.buildEdges is overloaded for ExplorationResult and ComparisonResult without distinguishing suffixes
issues.suggestion: Rename to buildExplorationEdges(ExplorationResult) and buildComparisonEdges(ComparisonResult) so callers can identify the correct variant without inspecting the argument type.
evidence: public static List<Edge> buildEdges(ExplorationResult result) — produces SEED_ADJ + DISCOVERED_ADJ edges
public static List<Edge> buildEdges(ComparisonResult result) — produces MODIFIER edges
Call sites must rely on type inference to identify which variant is called; the two variants produce structurally different edge sets with different RelationEdgeType values

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper uses the generic 'Helper' suffix despite owning specific collocate search execution and logDice ranking logic
issues.suggestion: Rename to CollocateSearchExecutor or CollocateRanker to communicate its role as the primary collocate lookup and scoring component.
evidence: CollocateQueryHelper owns: executeCollocateSearch(), buildAndRankCollocates(), getTotalFrequency() — these are not helper utilities but the core search and ranking operations
'Helper' suffix typically signals a utility bag with no cohesive identity; this class has a clear identity: it executes and ranks collocate searches for BlackLabQueryExecutor

issues.summary: ComparisonResult.fullyShared/partiallyShared/specific duplicate filtering logic already encoded in AdjectiveProfile.sharingCategory()
issues.suggestion: Remove fullyShared(), partiallyShared(), specific(), and specificTo() from ComparisonResult. Callers that need filtered subsets should filter allAdjectives() by adj.sharingCategory(). Keep summaryCounts() as it is a genuinely efficient single-pass count.
evidence: ComparisonResult.fullyShared(): filters adjectives where presentInCount == nouns.size() — same condition as AdjectiveProfile.isFullyShared()
ComparisonResult.partiallyShared(): filters where presentInCount >= 2 && presentInCount < nouns.size() — same condition as AdjectiveProfile.isPartiallyShared()
AdjectiveProfile already encodes the sharing category via sharingCategory() which returns a SharingCategory enum
The filter methods in ComparisonResult are only used externally in tests or assembler code; ExploreResponseAssembler currently uses allAdjectives() and then calls adj.sharingCategory()

issues.summary: SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() is a pure return pass-through with no added behavior or policy
issues.suggestion: Remove toBaseOptions() and update callers to use opts.base() directly, consistent with the refactoring direction already taken for the other three delegation methods.
evidence: public ExplorationOptions toBaseOptions() { return base; } — identical to accessing the base field directly
The recent change note says '3 pass-through delegation methods removed; callers use opts.base() directly' — toBaseOptions() appears to be a fourth that was retained

issues.summary: BlackLab 4.x resolved from an unstable GitHub raw-content URL with no availability SLA
issues.suggestion: Run the provided install-blacklab.sh script to install the JAR into a local Maven repository and remove the GitHub raw-content repository entry from pom.xml, or mirror the JAR to a private registry (Nexus/Artifactory/GitHub Packages). At minimum, commit the install script output to a local .m2 cache in CI to avoid transient network failures.
evidence: pom.xml repository entry: <url>https://raw.githubusercontent.com/instituutnederlandsetaal/BlackLab/mvn-repo/</url>
pom.xml comment: 'This URL has no SLA and can become unavailable (rate-limited, branch deleted, or repo renamed)'
BlackLab 4.x is not published to Maven Central; pom.xml acknowledges latest Central version is 3.0.1
CI builds would fail silently if this URL becomes unavailable with no local fallback

issues.summary: ConlluConverter uses long[] with implicit index semantics for multi-field mutable state instead of named fields
issues.suggestion: Replace long[] state with a private mutable ConversionState class or record with fields sentenceCount, tokenCount, chunkCount. Pass it by reference through processLines, eliminating the index magic and the confusing rotateChunkIfNeeded Optional<long[]> return value.
evidence: long[] state = {0, 0, 0}; // [sentences, tokens, chunks] — the comment is the only documentation for the magic indices
state[0]++, state[1]++, state[2] are accessed throughout processLines and rotateChunkIfNeeded
rotateChunkIfNeeded returns Optional.of(new long[]{chunks + 1}) and the caller assigns state[2] = rotated.get()[0] — the index coupling is fragile

issues.summary: ConlluConverter.processLines uses Optional<BufferedWriter> to represent nullable mutable writer state — non-idiomatic Java
issues.suggestion: Replace Optional<BufferedWriter> writerOpt with @Nullable BufferedWriter writer = null. Use explicit null checks (if (writer == null)) and direct assignment (writer = openChunk(...)). This is more idiomatic Java and makes the mutability explicit.
evidence: Optional<BufferedWriter> writerOpt = Optional.empty(); declared as a local variable reassigned throughout processLines
writerOpt = Optional.of(openChunk(...)): reassigning Optional wrappers in a loop is not idiomatic — Optional is designed for method return values, not mutable container state
if (writerOpt.isEmpty()) / writerOpt.get().write() called repeatedly — null check pattern via isEmpty/get is less readable than a plain @Nullable BufferedWriter with explicit null checks

issues.summary: GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition reimplements bracket-walking logic already available in CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens
issues.suggestion: Rewrite deriveTokenPosition to call CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens(pattern) and find the token index where the raw pattern text before the token starts with the label character + colon. This removes the duplicate bracket-walking and keeps bracket logic in one place.
evidence: GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition manually tracks bracket depth and scans for label characters (e.g. '1:') in a custom loop
CqlUtils.splitCqlTokens already walks brackets and returns an ordered token list — the same token list from which position can be derived by index
The CqlUtils.java Javadoc notes that GrammarConfigLoader.deriveTokenPosition 'extends this logic'

issues.summary: Explore response map is assembled across three sites: buildBaseExploreResponse, caller-added seed fields, and ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse
issues.suggestion: Consolidate all explore response assembly into ExploreResponseAssembler — add a buildSingleSeedResponse(ExplorationResult, ExploreParams) and buildMultiSeedResponse(ExplorationResult, ExploreParams) factory method in ExploreResponseAssembler, returning the complete response map. Handlers call a single method and get a complete, ready-to-send response.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExplore: Map<String, Object> response = buildBaseExploreResponse(...); response.put("seed", result.seed()); ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse(response, result);

handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: Map<String, Object> response = buildBaseExploreResponse(...); response.put("seeds", ...); response.put("seed_count", ...); ExploreResponseAssembler.populateExploreResponse(response, result);
The boundary between 'base', 'seed-specific', and 'exploration-data' fields is spread across ExplorationHandlers and ExploreResponseAssembler with no single assembly point

issues.summary: ExploreResponseAssembler.populateComparisonResponse receives seeds from both the Set<String> parameter and result.nouns(), sourcing the same concept from two different paths
issues.suggestion: Remove the Set<String> seeds parameter from populateComparisonResponse and derive seed count from result.nouns().size(). The method already has full information via the ComparisonResult parameter; the extra parameter creates a redundant and potentially inconsistent data path.
evidence: populateComparisonResponse signature: (Map<String,Object> response, ComparisonResult result, Set<String> seeds, int topCollocates, double minLogDice)
response.put("seeds", new ArrayList<>(result.nouns())) — uses result.nouns(), not the seeds parameter
response.put("seed_count", seeds.size()) — uses the seeds parameter, not result.nouns().size()
These two could diverge if result.nouns() is reordered or filtered vs. the seeds passed by the caller

issues.summary: Main.main() prints a decorative ASCII banner to stdout with no functional purpose
issues.suggestion: Remove the ASCII banner. Log startup information via the SLF4J logger (logger.info) instead, or omit it entirely. If a version string is needed, derive it from the JAR manifest rather than hardcoding it.
evidence: System.out.println("   ConceptSketch - BlackLab Edition v1.5.0 ")
Decorative banner printing is a common AI-generated boilerplate pattern; the version string duplicates the Maven pom.xml version and is a second source of truth

issues.summary: SingleSeedExplorationOptions.toBaseOptions() is a pure pass-through delegation method with no added behavior — AI-generated helper pattern
issues.suggestion: Remove toBaseOptions() and update any callers to access opts.base() directly, completing the cleanup already applied to the three sibling delegation methods.
evidence: public ExplorationOptions toBaseOptions() { return base; }
Recent changes note: '3 pass-through delegation methods removed; callers use opts.base() directly' — toBaseOptions() is a fourth delegation method that survived the cleanup
No caller benefits from toBaseOptions() vs opts.base() — the method adds no policy, translation, or safety

issues.summary: MultiSeedExplorer.computeCollocateIntersection retains Phase 1/2/3/4 step comments removed from SemanticFieldExplorer — annotating rather than explaining
issues.suggestion: Remove the // Phase N: comments from computeCollocateIntersection. The private phase-helper method names (fetchCollocatesPerSeed, identifyCommonCollocates, buildDiscoveredNouns, buildCoreCollocates) already describe each step — the comments are redundant.
evidence: // Phase 1: fetch collocates per seed
// Phase 2: identify common collocates meeting minShared threshold
// Phase 3: build aggregate score / frequency maps, then DiscoveredNoun list
// Phase 4: build core collocates
Same pattern was removed from SemanticFieldExplorer in the recent refactor; these comments annotate numbered steps rather than explain why the ordering or logic was chosen

issues.summary: Legacy /api/semantic-field route retained with untracked TODO lacking version or ticket reference
issues.suggestion: Add a version or GitHub issue reference to the TODO, e.g. '// TODO(#42): remove after v2.0 — canonical path is /api/semantic-field/compare'. Consider adding a deprecation header (Deprecation: true) in the handler's response to signal to clients that the endpoint is legacy.
evidence: Line 96: '// TODO: remove in a future version after clients have migrated to /api/semantic-field/compare.'
The TODO has no associated issue number, milestone, or version tag, making migration progress untrackable

issues.summary: api package conflates HTTP infrastructure (server, utils, exceptions) with five handler classes and a response assembler
issues.suggestion: Consider an api/handlers sub-package for the five handler classes and ExploreResponseAssembler, leaving WordSketchApiServer, HttpApiUtils, and RequestEntityTooLargeException in api as the HTTP infrastructure layer. This is a low-urgency improvement given the current scale.
evidence: api package contains: WordSketchApiServer, HttpApiUtils, RequestEntityTooLargeException (infrastructure), plus SketchHandlers, ExplorationHandlers, ConcordanceHandlers, CorpusQueryHandlers, VisualizationHandlers, ExploreResponseAssembler (application logic) — 10 classes with two distinct concerns
All classes are package-private, so the blurring has no external impact, but it makes the api package harder to navigate as it grows

issues.summary: ExplorationResult stores caller-supplied collections by reference, contradicting its 'immutable' Javadoc
issues.suggestion: Apply the same defensive-copy pattern used for 'seeds' to all mutable fields: 'this.seedCollocates = Map.copyOf(seedCollocates); this.seedCollocateFrequencies = Map.copyOf(seedCollocateFrequencies); this.discoveredNouns = List.copyOf(discoveredNouns); this.coreCollocates = List.copyOf(coreCollocates);'
evidence: Constructor: 'this.seedCollocates = seedCollocates;' — no defensive copy
Same for seedCollocateFrequencies, discoveredNouns, coreCollocates
Class Javadoc: 'All collections are non-null' implies stable read access but mutations by the caller are possible
seeds field IS correctly copied via 'this.seeds = List.copyOf(seeds)' — inconsistent treatment within the same constructor

issues.summary: CollocateProfileComparator.compareCollocateProfiles silently returns empty result for null or single-seed input without logging a warning
issues.suggestion: Add a logger.warn() before the early return: 'logger.warn("compareCollocateProfiles called with {} seeds (minimum 2); returning empty result", seedNouns == null ? 0 : seedNouns.size());' The calling path in SemanticFieldExplorer validates seeds >= 2 before this point, but defensive logging here aids debugging if the invariant is ever violated.
evidence: Method: 'if (seedNouns == null || seedNouns.size() < 2) { return ComparisonResult.empty(); }'
No log.warn() call before returning empty — callers that supply a single seed receive an empty result with no diagnostic

issues.summary: ConlluConverter.processLines relies on implicit 'inSentence implies writerOpt.isPresent()' invariant that is never asserted or documented
issues.suggestion: Add an assertion or an explicit isPresent check: 'if (inSentence && writerOpt.isPresent())' or assert the invariant with a comment. Alternatively, refactor to use a non-Optional local BufferedWriter field alongside a boolean inSentence flag to make the state machine explicit.
evidence: 'writerOpt.get().write("</s>\n")' inside 'if (inSentence)' block — gets value without isPresent check
The invariant holds because writerOpt is always set before inSentence is set to true, but this requires non-local reasoning to confirm
If the loop logic is ever refactored, this invariant can silently break with a NoSuchElementException at runtime

issues.summary: VisualizationHandlers enforces body-size with sendError+return while CorpusQueryHandlers throws RequestEntityTooLargeException, creating two divergent 413 patterns
issues.suggestion: Standardize on throwing RequestEntityTooLargeException in VisualizationHandlers to match CorpusQueryHandlers: 'if (bodyBytes.length > MAX_REQUEST_BODY_BYTES) { throw new RequestEntityTooLargeException("Request body too large"); }'. This keeps 413 logic in one place (wrapWithErrorHandling) and makes VisualizationHandlers consistent with the established pattern.
evidence: VisualizationHandlers: 'HttpApiUtils.sendError(exchange, 413, "Request body too large"); return;'
CorpusQueryHandlers: 'throw new RequestEntityTooLargeException("Request body too large");'
Only CorpusQueryHandlers path goes through wrapWithErrorHandling's 413 branch; VisualizationHandlers bypasses it with a direct sendError call

issues.summary: CollocateQueryHelper accepts @Nullable BlackLabIndex but only getTotalFrequency() guards against null; other methods NPE
issues.suggestion: Either: (1) remove @Nullable from the constructor parameter and document that test subclasses must override all methods that access index — making the null-safety contract explicit; or (2) add a requireNonNull guard in the constructor and create a separate test-only subclass that overrides the index-using methods and never calls super. Option 2 is cleaner as it eliminates the implicit contract.
evidence: Constructor Javadoc: 'Null is accepted to support test subclasses that override getTotalFrequency and executeCollocateSearch'
getTotalFrequency: 'Objects.requireNonNull(index, ...)' — correctly guarded
buildAndRankCollocates, executeBcqlQuery and similar methods call index.* directly — no null guard, NPE if called with null index
The @Nullable annotation implies null is safe to pass, but it is only safe for test subclasses that override the specific methods being invoked

issues.summary: ExplorationResult @NonNull collection accessors over-promise stability: underlying maps are not defensive copies
issues.suggestion: Apply defensive copies for all mutable collection fields: 'this.seedCollocates = Collections.unmodifiableMap(new LinkedHashMap<>(seedCollocates));' and similarly for the other fields. This matches the intent of the @NonNull annotations and the 'immutable result' documentation.
evidence: 'this.seedCollocates = seedCollocates;' — stores caller reference; seedCollocates() is annotated @NonNull but callers can mutate the map via the original reference
Contrast: 'this.seeds = List.copyOf(seeds);' — correctly defensive-copied
The Javadoc says 'All collections are non-null' but does not say 'unmodifiable', so the annotation is not technically false, but the asymmetry between seeds and the other collections is a latent bug

issues.summary: ExplorationParams always parses 'nouns_per' even in multi-seed path where it is pre-flight rejected, creating redundant logic
issues.suggestion: Introduce a multi-seed-specific request record (ValidatedMultiSeedRequest) that does not include 'nounsPerCollocate', or have parseExplorationParams accept a flag indicating whether to parse nouns_per. Simpler: leave the guard but add a comment explaining why parseExplorationParams still runs after it.
evidence: handleSemanticFieldExploreMulti: 'if (params.containsKey("nouns_per")) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("nouns_per is not supported for multi-seed exploration"); }'
buildExploreRequest calls parseExplorationParams which contains: 'int nounsPerCollocate = HttpApiUtils.parseIntParam(params, "nouns_per", 30);'

When nouns_per is absent the parse is a no-op (returns default 30), but when present the guard throws before reaching parse — the guard and the parse are logically coordinated only by execution order, not by structure

issues.summary: CorpusQueryHandlers 413 body-size limit, bracket-depth limit, and JSON parse error paths have no dedicated tests
issues.suggestion: Add tests to HandlersTest (or a new CorpusQueryHandlersTest) for: (1) body > 65536 → HTTP 413, (2) pattern.length() > 1024 → HTTP 400, (3) bracketCount > 20 → HTTP 400, (4) invalid JSON → HTTP 400. Use MockExchange with a ByteArrayInputStream for the POST body.
evidence: CorpusQueryHandlers.parseBcqlRequest has three distinct validation branches: body > 65536 bytes (413), pattern.length() > 1024 (400), bracketCount > 20 (400)
